data.title: Gold hits fresh record high after soft US jobs data
data.description:
Gold prices hit fresh record highs on Friday after a soft U.S. jobs report cemented hopes of a Federal Reserve interest rate cut, fuelling fresh momentum for bullion’s blistering rally.

Spot gold was up 0.9% at $3,577.33 per ounce, as of 12:34 GMT. Prices hit a record high of $3,582.71 and were up 3.7% so far this week.

U.S. gold futures for December delivery rose 0.9% to $3,637.00.

Data showed that U.S. job growth weakened sharply in August while the unemployment rate increased to 4.3%, confirming that labor market conditions were softening and sealing the case for an interest rate cut from the Federal Reserve this month.

Bullion, which doesn’t pay interest, tends to shine when rates are low and uncertainty is high, making it a go-to asset for investors seeking safety.

China and India are top gold consumers. Physical demand for gold in these hubs dropped this week due to record high prices.

August gold in reserves data from China's central bank, due on Sunday, will not catch the September record highs, but may still provide more clarity on how demand from central banks was being affected by high bullion prices.

Among other metals, spot silver rose 1% to $41.09 per ounce and was heading for its third straight weekly gain.

Platinum gained 2.2% to $1,396.97 and palladium was down 0.2% at $1,125.44.

data.title: This Customer Engagement Software Stock Is Up 23%. Here's Why. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Mackenzie Tatananni

Braze stock surged in premarket trading Friday after the customer engagement platform posted a solid second quarter and boosted its financial guidance.

Adjusted earnings of 15 cents a share came in above analysts' calls for three cents a share, according to FactSet. Revenue surged 28% from the prior year to $180.1 million, topping the $171.6 million Wall Street had forecast.

The software maker also more than doubled its forecast for its full-year profit. Braze sees adjusted earnings in the range of 41 cents to 42 cents a share, compared with its previous outlook of 15 cents to 18 cents and above the 17 cents analysts were looking for.

The company said it expects revenue between $717 million and $720 million, up from a prior range of $702 million to $706 million. Wall Street had anticipated $704.5 million.

Mizuho analysts noted Friday that Braze delivered a "strong beat and raise" in the latest quarter.

"Operating margin of 3.4% exceeded consensus of 0.7% and should alleviate investor concerns on margin expansion," the team wrote. Mizuho reiterated an Outperform rating on Braze shares and raised the price target slightly to $45 from $40.

The stock soared 23% to $34 on Friday. Futures tracking the benchmark S&P 500 and tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite were up 0.2% and 0.6%, respectively, following the release of the U.S. jobs report for August.

Citi Research, similarly, reiterated a Buy rating on the stock while boosting the price target to $52 from $50. The firm cited a "modest reacceleration" in organic revenue during the quarter.

Braze's strong performance appeared to be driven by better execution rather than industry trends, the analysts continued, which they believe highlights Braze's differentiation versus application software peers.

The print wasn't perfect. Oppenheimer analysts pointed out that net revenue retention — a measure of how well the company can retain revenue from existing customers over a 12-month period — came in at 108% for the period ended July 31. This compares to 114% for the same period last year and 109% sequentially.

"NRR has yet to find a bottom which leave tough optics," the analysts wrote. "However, the stabilizing in period NRR trends makes us optimistic that NRR expansion looms for Braze, and this development could catalyze higher multiples." Oppenheimer rates Braze at Outperform with a $38 target price.

Write to Mackenzie Tatananni at mackenzie.tatananni@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Federated Hermes Is Maintained at Outperform by Evercore ISI Group
US Dollar Declines after Jobs Report
data.description:
The US dollar index fell to 97.7 on Friday, tracking the drop in short-term Treasury yields after evidence of a more serious deterioration in the labor market flagged concerns on the US economy and supported bets of multiple rate cuts by the Fed. 22 thousand nonfarm payrolls were added in August, contrasting sharply with expectations of a 75 thousand increase, while the unemployment rate rose to a near four-year high of 4.3%.

The pessimistic data matched dovish remarks from FOMC members, who recently noted that rates should be adjusted to accommodate for a slowing economy and labor market.

The central bank is now expected to restart its cutting cycle this month and deliver three total cuts this year.

Conversely, employment growth was sustained in the Eurozone and the bloc's inflation ticked higher, driving rate traders to favor no more rate cuts by the ECB this year and lifting the euro, by far the largest currency in the DXY index.

data.title: TABLE - Karachi Cotton Association Prices
data.description:
The Karachi Cotton Association on Sept 4 announced the official spot rate, or base price, for Grade 3 cotton at 16,933 rupees per maund (40 kg).

Grade 3 cotton has a staple length of 1-1/16" and micronaire value between 3.8 and 4.9 NCL (no control limits), which represents fine to coarse classes of cotton varieties.

data.title: VinFast Auto Is Maintained at Buy by Chardan Capital
Broadcom to Begin Making Custom Chips for New Customer — Market Talk
data.description: Christmas came early for Broadcom, Oppenheimer analysts say in a research note. The semiconductor and software maker's 3Q results and 4Q sales outlook topped Wall Street expectations. But what really stole the show was the company's strong backlog, and its disclosure that it will add a fourth XPU customer in 2026. Broadcom already makes custom chips for Google, Meta and Bytedance, the analysts say, adding they believe the company is adding OpenAI to its roster next year. Of the company's remaining prospective customers, Broadcom says it could add another active customer by the end of 2026. Oppenheimer raises its price target to $360 from $325. Shares rise 14% to $349.07 premarket. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: S&P and Nasdaq Futures Hold Gains After Payrolls
data.description:
US stock futures remained mostly positive on Friday, with S&P 500 contracts up 0.2% and Nasdaq 100 futures gaining 0.6%, while Dow Jones futures hovered near the flatline as investors digested the latest jobs report.

The US economy added just 22K jobs in August, far below forecasts of 75K, while the unemployment rate rose to 4.3%, the highest since 2021 and in line with expectations.

The data reinforced signs of a cooling labor market and bolstered bets that the Fed will cut rates later this month, with markets now pricing in about 66bps of easing for 2025.

On the corporate side, Broadcom surged 14% in premarket trading after posting strong quarterly results, while Lululemon slumped nearly 20% after issuing weak guidance, citing tariff-related profit pressures.

For the week, the S&P 500 is up 0.7%, the Nasdaq 1.2%, and the Dow 0.2% so far.

data.title: Janus Henderson Group Is Maintained at In-Line by Evercore ISI Group
data.description: (12:55 GMT) Janus Henderson Group Price Target Raised to $48.00/Share From $45.00 by Evercore ISI Group

data.title: Invesco Is Maintained at In-Line by Evercore ISI Group
Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0854 ET - Christmas came early for Broadcom, Oppenheimer analysts say in a research note. The semiconductor and software maker's 3Q results and 4Q sales outlook topped Wall Street expectations. But what really stole the show was the company's strong backlog, and its disclosure that it will add a fourth XPU customer in 2026. Broadcom already makes custom chips for Google, Meta and Bytedance, the analysts say, adding they believe the company is adding OpenAI to its roster next year. Of the company's remaining prospective customers, Broadcom says it could add another active customer by the end of 2026. Oppenheimer raises its price target to $360 from $325. Shares rise 14% to $349.07 premarket. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0845 ET - FedEx and UPS have increased their peak season surcharges, but the level of seasonal shipping demand is still uncertain, say JPMorgan analysts. Downstream shipping inventory is currently increasing, "which implies there will be some seasonality-supported parcel demand into peak season," the analysts say, adding that e-commerce spending trends have remained strong. But a recent PWC survey found consumers plan to cut their seasonal spending by about 5%, including a 23% holiday spending cut among Gen Z consumers, the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0835 ET - FedEx is unlikely to see its business mix shift to higher-margin services any time soon, say J.P. Morgan analysts. Since the end of the pandemic, the carrier market has moved to lower-margin mix with business-to-business demand pressured amid a "muted industrial backdrop," leaving business-to-consumer the only segment seeing growth. FedEx has leaned into this shift, increasing its volume of short-distance, light-weight packages to boost its business-to-consumer share. "The onus will be on FedEx to prove it can generate strong incremental margins on the back of such B2C growth as we remain skeptical this strategy can generate meaningful operating leverage," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0816 ET - The early removal of the non-China de minimis exemption won't impact FedEx's F1Q results slated for September 18, but will likely present longer term challenges for the company, say JPMorgan analysts. The removal could lead to decreased shipments and a risk to incremental revenue, which implies that the expected $170 million tariff headwind could worsen throughout the year. FedEx has noted that shipments of higher-value goods closer to the $800 de minimis threshold are more resilient, but it's "seen more demand destruction on lower value goods," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0802 ET - Tesla CEO Elon Musk would receive more than 423.7 million shares of the company's common stock, worth a preliminary aggregate fair value estimate of $87.75 billion, under a newly proposed pay package, assuming he grows the company's market capitalization to at least $8.5 trillion by 2035, according to a proxy statement filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission. These shares would be paid out in 12 tranches. The first tranche milestone is a market capitalization of $2 trillion, and the next nine tranches thereafter each require an additional $500 billion in market capitalization, up to $6.5 trillion. The last two tranches each require an additional $1 trillion in market capitalization. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0757 ET - Not allowing Abu Dhabi's state oil group ADNOC to buy German chemicals maker Covestro would be a mistake for the European Union's competition regulator, Citi says in an note. The bank says it had concerns the watchdog was focusing on a planned capital increase when it decided to open an in-depth investigation into the deal under rules governing subsidies from non-EU governments. "Not approving this capital raise could be a deal-breaker, in our view," it says. "Deterring investors, who see value in the assets and want to invest in them, cannot be helpful," it adds. (edith.hancock@wsj.com)

0756 ET - Tesla's board is asking shareholders to approve a performance award for CEO Elon Musk worth a preliminary aggregate fair value estimate of $87.75 billion. Musk would only get the full proposed pay package if he creates $7.5 trillion in value. The company says it is at a critical inflection point as it shifts focus from electric vehicles and renewable energy industries, toward artificial intelligence and robotics. "We believe that Elon's singular vision is vital to navigating this critical inflection point," it says in a recent filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission. "Simply put, retaining and incentivizing Elon is fundamental to Tesla achieving these goals and becoming the most valuable company in history." Shares rise 2% premarket. (connor.hart@wsj.com) (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0743 ET - Challenges from domestic market pressures and the end of the de minimis import exemption fueled a significant cut to Lululemon's FY25 guidance, say Stifel's Peter McGoldrick and Alexander Douglas. The analysts downgrade the stock to hold from buy and reduce the target price to $205 from $324. Lululemon is seeing "signs of loyal customer attrition" and its effort to regain U.S. momentum will take longer than they previously anticipated, the analysts say. The removal of the de minimis exemption created "greater-than-anticipated headwinds to profitability," the analysts add. Lululemon tumbles 19% to $166.90 premarket. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0730 ET - U.K. assets including sterling, government bonds and stocks are little moved after U.K. Deputy Prime Minister Angela Rayner said she would resign after failing to pay enough tax on a property purchase. The move was widely anticipated with an independent ethics advisor looking into whether she broke ministerial standard rules. The government is already facing a challenging outlook with rising debt levels ahead of the autumn budget on Nov. 26. Fiscal sustainability concerns sent long-dated bond yields surging earlier in the week, denting sterling. The currency rises 0.3% to $1.3475, little changed after the news. Ten-year and 30-year gilt yields are steady at 4.700% and 5.551%. respectively, Tradeweb data show. The FTSE 100 rises 0.3% to 9243 points. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0728 ET - Lululemon knows it has to refresh its lines, and until it does, Americans aren't likely going to flock to its stores, according to Citi. For several years, the only newness that it introduced into the core and casual lines were new colors and seasonal updates, not actual new products, which is what the customer is looking for, the analysts say in a note. They add that management is pointing to spring '26 for better performance in the Americas driven by newness moving from 23% to 35% and a new product team's full influence over the assortment. The analysts say that given recent underperformance, the changes "will likely be viewed skeptically." Lululemon sinks 18% premarket. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0704 ET - French banking shares should continue to recover losses incurred following a recent selloff, RBC Capital Markets says in a research note. Stocks slid recently after the announcement of a confidence vote in the government, but they should recover even if the vote passes on Monday. Shares might react but should move on quickly to ignore political risks, analysts add. "There does not appear to be an easy fix on how the political impasse can be overcome to provide more visibility on the economic outlook," analysts note. After the vote, focus will shift to risks on the horizon, which include the failure of agreeing on a 2026 budget by year-end to avoid further widening bond spreads and a hit to economic prospects. This can hurt banks' performance though their diversification somewhat insulates them from such risks, RBC says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0650 ET - Sixt is a best-in-breed car-rental company that will expand faster than expected in the U.S., write UBS analysts. The German company's U.S. market share will climb to 5% by 2029 from 3% currently, they say. Positive trends point to a more rapid expansion than market concerns over a slowdown suggest, the analysts note. Sixt's revenue is expected to rise 8% from 2025 through 2029, and Sixt will post 11-13% earnings before interest and taxes between 2025 and 2027, they say. This compares with company guidance of 10% for the current year. Analysts also highlight Sixt's success in cutting costs and thereby boosting margins to 13%. UBS starts coverage of Sixt with a buy recommendation and a target price of 102 euros. Shares in Sixt trade 3.4% higher at 83.40 euros. (william.gray@wsj.com)

data.title: Canada's economy sheds 65,500 jobs in August
data.description:
Canada's economy lost 65,500 jobs in August and the jobless rate fell to 7.1%, a nine-year high outside the pandemic years, Statistics Canada data showed on Friday.

Market reaction:

Link: https://www150.statcan.gc.ca/n1/daily-quotidien/250905/dq250905a-eng.htm?HPA=1&indid=3587-2&indgeo=0

COMMENTARY

ANDREW KELVIN, HEAD OF CANADIAN AND GLOBAL RATES STRATEGY, TD SECURITIES

"It's a very poor number, there's no sugar coating it. If you look at the underlying details it's perhaps not as soft as the headline number would suggest, but that being said the headline number is extremely weak."

"The job losses were concentrated in part-time positions but we still lost full-time positions. The unemployment rate is now above 7%, there is clearly slack in the economy. I do think it's something the Bank of Canada is going to look at as supporting further policy easing ... We do expect them to cut rates this month."

DOUG PORTER, CHIEF ECONOMIST AT BMO CAPITAL MARKETS

"Obviously, (the report is) weaker than expected. I would say this is a more clear-cut case of softness than the July report, which I viewed as a correction to the surprising strength in June. This one seems to be fairly weak across the board. It might not be quite as bad as the headlines suggested, because a lot of the losses were in part-time positions and self-employment, but still, you know, the reality is the unemployment rate has pushed above 7%. This is the second double-digit drop in jobs in a row. ... We always try to do a quick rating based on the many different indicators and this report is a fail."

data.title: Canada Unemployment Rate Rises to 7.1% in August
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--The unemployment rate in Canada rose more than expected in August, hitting its highest since May 2016 outside of the height of the pandemic.

Statistics Canada reported Friday that Canada's economy lost a net 65,500 jobs last month, building on the 40,800 drop the month before. Market expectations were for employment to rise by a modest 10,000.

The unemployment rate advanced to 7.1%, the data agency said. Expectations were for the jobless rate to tick up to 7% after holding steady at 6.9% in July.

The decline in employment in August was largely due to a fall in part-time work.

The agency said there were fewer people working in professional, scientific and technical services during the month. Employment also fell in transportation and warehousing, and in manufacturing, it said.

When calculated using U.S. Labor Department methodology, Canada's unemployment rate last month climbed to 6% from 5.8%.

Write to Robb M. Stewart robb.stewart@wsj.com

data.title: Hello Group Inc expected to post earnings of CNY2.11 a share - Earnings Preview
data.description:
• Hello Group Inc is expected to show a fall in quarterly revenue when it reports results on September 9 for the period ending June 30 2025

  • The Beijing-based company is expected to report a 3.0% decrease in revenue to CNY2.611 billion from CNY2.69 billion a year ago, according to the mean estimate from 5 analysts, based on LSEG data.The company's guidance on June 5 2025, for the period ended June 30, was for revenue between CNY2.57 billion and CNY2.67 billion.

  • ​LSEG's mean analyst estimate for Hello Group Inc is for earnings of CNY2.11 per share.

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 7 "strong buy" or "buy," 1 "hold" and 1 "sell" or "strong sell."

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts was unchanged in the last three months. ​

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Hello Group Inc is $9.30, about 10.2% above its last closing price of $8.35

data.title: Canadian Wage Growth Rises Again in August — Market Talk
data.description: While consumer inflation in Canada has largely calmed, wage growth is looking sticky. Annual CPI eased to 1.7% in July and has been below the Bank of Canada's 2% four months running. But pay has been ticking up. In August, average hourly wages for permanent employees rose 3.6% after advancing to 3.5% the month before. That was stronger than the 3.4% growth economists expected. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

data.title: Traders See Chance of Half-Point Rate Cut After Weak Payrolls — WSJ
data.description:
By Jack Pitcher

Traders in interest-rate derivatives now see a 100% chance the Federal Reserve will cut benchmark interest rates this month, including a 12% chance of a larger, half-percentage-point cut, according to CME Group.

Wall Street assigned overwhelming odds to a September cut before the latest data, but no chance of a supersized cut of 0.5 percentage point, or 50 basis points.

"A 50 basis-point cut is back on the table. Everyone was probably more keyed-in on the revisions than the headline number. With revisions, there was nearly net zero job creation," said Brian Jacobsen, chief economist at Annex Wealth Management.

Short-term Treasury yields dropped sharply after the report.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Canada Unemployment Rate Rises to 7.1% in August — Update
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--Canada shed jobs in August for a second month running, pushing the unemployment rate to its highest level in years as weakness in the labor market looks to have broadened.

Employers in the country cut a net 65,500 jobs last month, Statistics Canada said Friday. That builds on the 40,800 jobs shed the month before to leave the unemployment rate 0.2 percentage point higher at 7.1%.

The jobless rate now sits at its highest level since May 2016, outside of the peak during the pandemic lockdowns in 2020 and 2021. Economists were expecting an August unemployment rate of 7%, and for employment to have steadied with a slight rise of about 10,000.

When calculated using U.S. Labor Department methodology, Canada's unemployment rate was 0.2 point higher at 6%.

The data, the last labor survey before the Bank of Canada's governing council meets later this month, is likely to lift expectations that interest rates could be cut to help support an economy that contracted more than anticipated in the second quarter.

For a second month the data showed job losses beyond just the industries directly exposed to trade and higher tariffs. Following five months of little change, there were 26,100 fewer people employed in professional, scientific and technical-services roles, representing a 1.3% pullback from the month before. That follows a 3.3% drop in job in July in information, culture and recreation positions.

The pressures of tariffs and trade uncertainty continue to show in the data. Jobs in manufacturing declined by 19,200 in August, and have fallen roughly 58,000 from a recent peak in January. Employment in transportation and warehousing also dropped by 22,700, offsetting a similar-sized increase the month before.

Unlike in July, when job losses were concentrated in younger workers 24 and under and in full-time positions, the employment decline in August was felt largely by part-time workers and by adults 25 to 54 years old.

Business and household sentiment remains subdued amid worries about trade policy and the fallout of tariffs on prices and demand.

The Canadian Federation of Small Business's latest monthly survey found little appetite for new staffing, with plans to hire part-time workers remaining negative and with only slightly more employers looking to add full-time roles than those planning to reduce them. At the same time, the federal government has warned job losses in the public sector are likely as it looks to tighten spending.

Total hours worked were little changed from a month prior, ticking up 0.1% in August and rising 0.9% on a year earlier.

Wage growth also again heated up slightly and continues to outpace annual consumer price inflation, which cooled to 1.7% in July. Wages for permanent employees rose 3.6% on a year earlier, up from a 3.5% advance the month before and topping the 3.4% rise economists expected.

There were 1.6 million unemployed Canadians in August, and the layoff rate edged up to 1% for the month, compared with 0.9% 12 months earlier. Those who were unemployed in July showed signs of struggling to find work in August, with just 15.2% of those without a job finding work versus a rate of 23.3% in the same months from 2017 to 2019.

The participation rate--the proportion of the population aged 15 and older who were employed or looking for work--fell by 0.1 point to 65.1% in August.

Write to Robb M. Stewart robb.stewart@wsj.com

data.title: Long-Term Unemployment Worsens — Market Talk
data.description: The share of unemployed workers who have been out of a job for more than six months increased in August. Last month, 26% of unemployed people had been hunting for half a year or more, up from 25% in July. The figure has been steadily climbing for much of the year so far, up from 21% last August. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)
